docs.title: Sockcan
docs.text: Sockcan is a fast and lightweight Socketcan implementation for Python. SocketCAN is an implementation of CAN protocols within the Linux kernel that provides a unified interface over a standard socket API. This library has two main goals: providing a performant client-side side implementation (in comparison of python-can), and providing a server side implementation to run SocketCAN-alike communications on Windows systems (which do not have socketCAN natively).

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text:
sockcan is available on PyPI:

pip install sockcan

docs.title: Cross-Platform Compatibility with Userspace CAN
docs.text:
This library provides an implementation of a SocketCAN server, which can run on systems like Windows that do not have native SocketCAN support. By running in userspace, Sockcan unlocks several critical capabilities:

  • Concurrency on the Bus: Overcome the limitations of single-threaded CAN bus access found in drivers like PCAN, enabling multiple applications or threads to interact with the CAN bus simultaneously.
  • Virtual CAN: Create and manage virtual CAN interfaces on interfaces that do not provide them natively, facilitating isolated testing environments, simulation, and development without requiring physical CAN hardware.

docs.title: Performance and Efficiency
docs.text: python-can is great at providing an common abstract interface over many CAN drivers, but not so great when it comes to performance. sockcan implementation of SocketCAN should run 3-4x faster than python-can's one. Sockcan provides an interfaces that's partially compatible with python-can, and can even replace the python-can's implementation by a faster one with a one-liner.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
Explore our tutorials to quickly get up and running with Sockcan.

Getting Started with Sockcan

docs.title: Core Concepts of Sockcan
docs.text: Sockcan is built around a few fundamental concepts that enable its high performance, flexibility, and python-can compatibility. Understanding these concepts is key to effectively using the library.

docs.title: 1. SocketCAN-alike Protocol
docs.text: At its heart, Sockcan implements a protocol that mimics the Linux SocketCAN interface. This allows it to interact with CAN devices in a standardized way, even on platforms that don't natively support SocketCAN.

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text:
• CanMessage: A dataclass that serves as a container for CAN message data. It includes arbitration_id, data, is_extended_id, and timestamp, closely matching python-can's Message object for compatibility.
  • SocketcanConfig: A dataclass used to configure the SocketCAN connection, including the channel (e.g., 'can0') and loopback mode.

docs.title: 2. Loopback Modes
docs.text: Sockcan provides different loopback modes to control whether transmitted messages are also received by the sending socket or by other sockets on the same CAN device.

docs.title: LoopbackMode Enum
docs.text:
• OFF: Transmitted messages are not received by any socket (including the sender).
  • FOR_OTHER_SOCKS: (Default) Transmitted messages are received by other sockets connected to the same CAN device but not by the sender.
  • ON: Transmitted messages are received by the sending socket itself, as well as other connected sockets.

docs.title: 3. Message Sending and Receiving Functions
docs.text: Sockcan provides flexible functions to build send and receive operations, allowing for optimized message handling.

docs.title: connect_to_socketcan(config: SocketcanConfig) -> SocketcanFd
docs.text: This function creates and configures a socket.socket object based on the provided SocketcanConfig, returning a SocketcanFd ready for use.

docs.title: build_recv_func(fd: SocketcanFd, *, use_native_timestamps: bool = True, is_stream: bool = False) -> RecvFn
docs.text: This factory function constructs a receive function (RecvFn) for a given SocketcanFd. It can be configured to use native hardware timestamps (if available and use_native_timestamps is True) and to operate in a stream-like manner (is_stream). The returned RecvFn is a callable that, when invoked, returns a CanMessage.

docs.title: build_send_func(fd: SocketcanFd, *, expects_msg_cls: bool = False) -> SendFn | MessageSendFn
docs.text: This factory function creates a send function (SendFn or MessageSendFn) for a SocketcanFd. If expects_msg_cls is True, the returned function expects a CanMessageProtocol object; otherwise, it expects individual arbitration_id, data, and is_extended parameters.

docs.title: What is Sockcan?
docs.text: Sockcan is a Python library designed to offer a performant and flexible interface for CAN (Controller Area Network) communication. It aims to provide a compatible API with the widely used python-can library but with a focus on speed and cross-platform capabilities through a userspace implementation.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• High Performance: Optimized for faster message processing and lower latency compared to traditional python-can setups.
  • Userspace Implementation: Enables CAN communication on platforms like Windows, where native SocketCAN is unavailable, by simulating SocketCAN behavior in userspace.
  • python-can Compatible Interface: Minimizes migration effort for existing python-can applications.
  • Concurrency: Supports concurrent access to the CAN bus, allowing multiple parts of an application to send and receive messages simultaneously.
  • Virtual CAN: Facilitates the creation of virtual CAN interfaces for development, testing, and simulation without physical hardware.
  • CAN to TCP/UDP bridge: the userspace SocketCAN server provided by this package can bridge to both TCP and UDP communications, depending on your configuration.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Well-suited for performance constrained environements. Embedded systems running Linux (e.g., Raspberry Pi) might not even be able to process a full bus-load without having CPU throttling with python-can.
  • Ease porting Linux implementations to Windows: can allow running a Linux SocketCAN's based application on Windows by simply running the userspace SocketCAN server provided by this package on top of the real bus. This can enable bus concurrency and virtualizaion on Windows, which are not available natively with PeakCAN drivers.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Timestamps: Timestamps in SocketCAN rely on socket ancillary data, which does not exit on Windows. Thus, to keep compatibility with SocketCAN, built-in timesdtamps (which in SocketCAN would be coming from the CAN driver directly) have to be disabled, and timestamping is done at the netowrk-level (thus less accurate, and also subject to any lag in the reception chain).
  • Filters: CAN filtering is currently only partially supported. You should make sure you application logic can reject properly unwanted messages.
  • Blocking policy: custom timeouts in receive methods are not support yet. When using selector protocols over the socket, receive is only non-blocking if both sides implement properly the protocol, and might currently end-up blocking in case of unexpected payload.

docs.title: Advanced Usage
docs.text: This guide covers advanced topics in Sockcan, including loopback modes, extended CAN IDs, filters, and interoperability with python-can.

docs.title: 1. Configuring Loopback Modes
docs.text: Sockcan allows you to control how transmitted messages are looped back to the sending socket or other sockets on the same CAN device.

docs.title: Standalone Mode
docs.text:
from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan
from sockcan._protocol import LoopbackMode, build_send_func, build_recv_func
import time

# Example 1: LoopbackMode.FOR_OTHER_SOCKS (Default)
# Messages are received by other sockets, but not the sender.
print("\n--- LoopbackMode.FOR_OTHER_SOCKS (Default) ---")
config_for_others = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0", loopback=LoopbackMode.FOR_OTHER_SOCKS)
sock1 = connect_to_socketcan(config_for_others)
sock2 = connect_to_socketcan(config_for_others)

try:
    send_func1 = build_send_func(sock1, expects_msg_cls=False)
    recv_func1 = build_recv_func(sock1)
    recv_func2 = build_recv_func(sock2)

    message_data = b'\x11\x22'
    send_func1(0x100, message_data, False)
    print("Sent message with ID 0x100 from sock1.")

    # sock2 SHOULD receive the message
    received_by_sock2 = recv_func2()
    print(f"Sock2 received: ID={received_by_sock2.arbitration_id:#x}, Data={received_by_sock2.data.hex()}")

finally:
    sock1.close()
    sock2.close()

# Example 2: LoopbackMode.ON
# Messages are received by the sending socket itself.
print("\n--- LoopbackMode.ON ---")
config_on = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0", loopback=LoopbackMode.ON)
sock_loopback_on = connect_to_socketcan(config_on)

try:
    send_func_on = build_send_func(sock_loopback_on, expects_msg_cls=False)
    recv_func_on = build_recv_func(sock_loopback_on)

    message_data = b'\xAA\xBB'
    send_func_on(0x200, message_data, False)
    print("Sent message with ID 0x200 from sock_loopback_on.")

    received_self = recv_func_on()
    print(f"Sock_loopback_on received its own message: ID={received_self.arbitration_id:#x}, Data={received_self.data.hex()}")

finally:
    sock_loopback_on.close()

# Example 3: LoopbackMode.OFF
# Messages are not received by any socket (sender or others).
print("\n--- LoopbackMode.OFF ---")
config_off = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0", loopback=LoopbackMode.OFF)
sock_loopback_off = connect_to_socketcan(config_off)
sock_other = connect_to_socketcan(config_off)

try:
    send_func_off = build_send_func(sock_loopback_off, expects_msg_cls=False)
    recv_func_other = build_recv_func(sock_other)

    message_data = b'\xCC\xDD'
    send_func_off(0x300, message_data, False)
    print("Sent message with ID 0x300 from sock_loopback_off.")
    print("Neither socket should receive the message in LoopbackMode.OFF")

finally:
    sock_loopback_off.close()
    sock_other.close()

docs.title: python-can Interoperability Mode
docs.text:
Loopback can be configured through python-can's receive_own_messages parameter:

import can
from sockcan.interop import hijack_python_can

hijack_python_can()

# receive_own_messages=True corresponds to LoopbackMode.ON
bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="vcan0", receive_own_messages=True)

docs.title: 2. Sending Extended CAN ID Messages
docs.text: Extended CAN IDs (29-bit) are supported by setting the is_extended flag to True.

docs.title: Standalone Mode
docs.text:
from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan
from sockcan._protocol import build_send_func, build_recv_func

config = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0")
sock_tx = connect_to_socketcan(config)
sock_rx = connect_to_socketcan(config)

try:
    send_func = build_send_func(sock_tx, expects_msg_cls=False)
    recv_func = build_recv_func(sock_rx)

    extended_arbitration_id = 0x18DA00F1  # Common extended ID example (OBD-II)
    extended_data = b'\x0A\x0B\x0C\x0D\x0E\x0F\x10\x11'
    is_extended = True

    send_func(extended_arbitration_id, extended_data, is_extended)
    print(f"Sent extended message: ID={extended_arbitration_id:#x}, Data={extended_data.hex()}")

    received_message = recv_func()
    print("Received Extended Message:")
    print(f"  Arbitration ID: {received_message.arbitration_id:#x}")
    print(f"  Data: {received_message.data.hex()}")
    print(f"  Is Extended ID: {received_message.is_extended_id}")

finally:
    sock_tx.close()
    sock_rx.close()

docs.title: python-can Interoperability Mode
docs.text:
import can
from sockcan.interop import hijack_python_can

hijack_python_can()

bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="vcan0")

# Extended ID message
msg = can.Message(
    arbitration_id=0x18DA00F1,
    data=[0x02, 0x01, 0x0D, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00],
    is_extended_id=True,
)
bus.send(msg)
print(f"Sent extended ID: {msg.arbitration_id:#x}")

docs.title: Standalone Mode
docs.text:
Use the SocketcanServer.subscribe() method with filter sets:

from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan
from sockcan._protocol import build_send_func, build_recv_func
from sockcan.daemon import SocketcanServer

# Create a server with message filtering
server = SocketcanServer(use_stream=True)
server.start()

# Subscribe with filters - only receive messages with IDs 0x100-0x1FF
sock = server.subscribe(filters={0x100, 0x101, 0x102, 0x200})

recv_func = build_recv_func(sock)
send_func = build_send_func(sock, expects_msg_cls=False)

# These will be received
send_func(0x100, b'\x01\x02', False)
msg = recv_func()
print(f"Received filtered: ID={msg.arbitration_id:#x}")

# This will be filtered out
send_func(0x999, b'\x03\x04', False)
# msg = recv_func()  # Would block - message was filtered

docs.title: python-can Interoperability Mode
docs.text:
Use python-can's can_filters parameter:

import can
from sockcan.interop import hijack_python_can

hijack_python_can()

# Filter to only receive messages with IDs 0x100-0x1FF
bus = can.Bus(
    interface="socketcan",
    channel="vcan0",
    can_filters=[{"can_id": 0x100, "can_mask": 0x700}],  # ID range 0x100-0x1FF
)

msg = bus.recv()
print(f"Received: ID={msg.arbitration_id:#x}")

docs.title: 4. Using the Daemon for Concurrent Access
docs.text: For interfaces that don't support concurrent access (like PCAN on Windows), use the SocketcanServer to multiplex connections:

docs.title: Standalone Mode
docs.text:
from sockcan.daemon import SocketcanServer, BusParameters
from sockcan import build_send_func, build_recv_func

# Create a server wrapping real hardware
with SocketcanServer.factory(
    BusParameters(channel="PCAN_USBBUS1", interface="pcan", bitrate=500000)
) as server:
    # Multiple sockets can subscribe
    sock1 = server.subscribe()
    sock2 = server.subscribe()

    send1 = build_send_func(sock1, expects_msg_cls=False)
    send2 = build_send_func(sock2, expects_msg_cls=False)
    recv1 = build_recv_func(sock1)
    recv2 = build_recv_func(sock2)

    # Both sockets can send and receive concurrently
    send1(0x100, b'\x01\x02', False)
    send2(0x200, b'\x03\x04', False)

    # Both receive all messages
    msg1 = recv1()
    msg2 = recv2()
    print(f"Sock1 received: {msg1.arbitration_id:#x}")
    print(f"Sock2 received: {msg2.arbitration_id:#x}")

docs.title: python-can Interoperability Mode
docs.text:
Use activate_userspace_socketcan():

import can
from sockcan.interop import activate_userspace_socketcan, SocketcanDaemonConfig
from sockcan.daemon import BusParameters

config = SocketcanDaemonConfig(mode="local")
activate_userspace_socketcan(
    BusParameters(channel="PCAN_USBBUS1", interface="pcan", bitrate=500000),
    config=config,
)

# Multiple python-can buses can now share the same hardware
bus1 = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="PCAN_USBBUS1")
bus2 = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="PCAN_USBBUS1")

# Both buses share the same hardware concurrently
bus1.send(can.Message(arbitration_id=0x100, data=[1, 2]))
bus2.send(can.Message(arbitration_id=0x200, data=[3, 4]))

docs.title: 5. Virtual vs Real CAN Interface
docs.text: All examples in this guide use vcan0 (virtual CAN). To use real hardware:

docs.title: Standalone Mode
docs.text:
# Virtual CAN
config = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0")

# Real CAN hardware
config = SocketcanConfig(channel="can0")

docs.title: python-can Mode
docs.text:
# Virtual CAN
bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="vcan0")

# Real CAN hardware
bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="can0")

docs.title: Daemon Mode with Real Hardware
docs.text:
from sockcan.daemon import SocketcanServer, BusParameters

# Linux native socketcan
with SocketcanServer.factory(
    BusParameters(channel="can0", interface="socketcan")
) as server:
    sock = server.subscribe()

# PCAN on Windows
with SocketcanServer.factory(
    BusParameters(channel="PCAN_USBBUS1", interface="pcan", bitrate=500000)
) as server:
    sock = server.subscribe()

docs.title: 6. Context Manager Usage
docs.text: Both standalone and interoperability modes support context managers for automatic cleanup:

docs.title: Standalone Mode
docs.text:
from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan, build_send_func, build_recv_func

with connect_to_socketcan(SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0")) as sock:
    send = build_send_func(sock, expects_msg_cls=False)
    recv = build_recv_func(sock)

    send(0x100, b'\x01\x02', False)
    msg = recv()
    print(f"Received: {msg.arbitration_id:#x}")

# Socket automatically closed on exit

docs.title: python-can Mode
docs.text:
import can
from sockcan.interop import hijack_python_can

hijack_python_can()

with can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="vcan0") as bus:
    msg = can.Message(arbitration_id=0x100, data=[1, 2])
    bus.send(msg)
    response = bus.recv()

# Bus automatically shutdown on exit

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text: This guide demonstrates the fundamental steps to set up a CAN bus connection, send messages, and receive messages using Sockcan.

docs.title: Two Usage Modes
docs.text:
Sockcan can be used in two main ways, as a 0-dependency library, or as an extension of python-can:

Mode Description Best For Standalone Direct use of Sockcan's API Maximum performance, Linux native python-can Drop-in Replaces python-can's socketcan backend Existing python-can applications, cross-platform, applications that must work over a variety of CAN drivers.

Usage below documents the basic API for both use cases.

docs.title: Standalone
docs.text:
Connect directly to a CAN interface using Sockcan's API:

from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan

# Configure the CAN channel and loopback mode
config = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0")

# Connect to the SocketCAN interface
sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)
print(f"Connected to CAN bus on channel: {config.channel}")

# Remember to close the socket when you're done
sock.close()

docs.title: With python-can
docs.text:
Replace python-can's socketcan implementation with Sockcan's optimized version:

import can
from sockcan.interop import hijack_python_can

# Replace python-can's socketcan backend
hijack_python_can()

# Now uses Sockcan internally
bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="vcan0")
print(f"Connected to: {bus.channel_info}")

bus.shutdown()

docs.title: Standalone
docs.text:
Build a send function to transmit CAN messages:

from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan
from sockcan._protocol import build_send_func

config = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0")
sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)

try:
    send_func = build_send_func(sock, expects_msg_cls=False)

    # Example CAN message data
    arbitration_id = 0x123
    data = b'\x01\x02\x03\x04'
    is_extended = False

    # Send the message
    send_func(arbitration_id, data, is_extended)
    print(f"Sent message: ID={arbitration_id:#x}, Data={data.hex()}")

finally:
    sock.close()

docs.title: With python-can
docs.text:
Use python-can's familiar API:

import can
from sockcan.interop import hijack_python_can

hijack_python_can()

bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="vcan0")

try:
    message = can.Message(
        arbitration_id=0x456,
        data=[0x05, 0x06, 0x07, 0x08],
        is_extended_id=False,
    )
    bus.send(message)
    print(f"Sent message: ID={message.arbitration_id:#x}")

finally:
    bus.shutdown()

docs.title: Standalone
docs.text:
Build a receive function that blocks until a message arrives:

from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan
from sockcan._protocol import build_recv_func

config = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0")
sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)

try:
    recv_func = build_recv_func(sock)

    print("Waiting for a CAN message...")
    received_message = recv_func()

    print("Received Message:")
    print(f"  Arbitration ID: {received_message.arbitration_id:#x}")
    print(f"  Data: {received_message.data.hex()}")
    print(f"  Is Extended ID: {received_message.is_extended_id}")
    print(f"  Timestamp: {received_message.timestamp}")

finally:
    sock.close()

docs.title: WIth python-can
docs.text:
Use python-can's recv() method:

import can
from sockcan.interop import hijack_python_can

hijack_python_can()

bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="vcan0")

try:
    print("Waiting for a CAN message...")
    message = bus.recv(timeout=5.0)  # 5 second timeout

    if message:
        print("Received Message:")
        print(f"  Arbitration ID: {message.arbitration_id:#x}")
        print(f"  Data: {message.data.hex()}")
        print(f"  Is Extended ID: {message.is_extended_id}")
    else:
        print("Timeout - no message received")

finally:
    bus.shutdown()

docs.title: Linux: Virtual CAN (vcan0)
docs.text:
sudo modprobe vcan
sudo ip link add dev vcan0 type vcan
sudo ip link set up vcan0

docs.title: Linux: Real CAN Hardware
docs.text:
# List available interfaces
ip link show

# Bring up your CAN interface
sudo ip link set up can0

docs.title: Windows
docs.text: Windows requires the userspace daemon mode. See Windows Setup for details.

docs.title: Standalone: TX/RX Application
docs.text:
from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan, build_send_func, build_recv_func
import time

def can_app(channel="vcan0"):
    config = SocketcanConfig(channel=channel)
    sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)
    send = build_send_func(sock, expects_msg_cls=False)
    recv = build_recv_func(sock)

    try:
        counter = 0
        while True:
            # Send periodic messages
            data = bytes([counter % 256])
            send(0x100, data, False)
            print(f"TX: ID=0x100, Data={data.hex()}")

            # Check for incoming messages
            try:
                msg = recv()
                print(f"RX: ID={msg.arbitration_id:#x}, Data={msg.data.hex()}")
            except BlockingIOError:
                pass

            counter += 1
            time.sleep(0.1)
    finally:
        sock.close()

docs.title: python-can: TX/RX Application
docs.text:
import can
from sockcan.interop import hijack_python_can

def can_app(channel="vcan0"):
    hijack_python_can()
    bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel=channel)

    try:
        counter = 0
        while True:
            # Send periodic messages
            msg = can.Message(
                arbitration_id=0x100,
                data=[counter % 256],
                is_extended_id=False,
            )
            bus.send(msg)
            print(f"TX: ID=0x{msg.arbitration_id:x}, Data={msg.data.hex()}")

            # Check for incoming messages
            received = bus.recv(timeout=0.01)
            if received:
                print(f"RX: ID={received.arbitration_id:#x}, Data={received.data.hex()}")

            counter += 1
    finally:
        bus.shutdown()

docs.title: connect_to_socketcan(config: SocketcanConfig) -> SocketcanFd
docs.text:
Creates a socketcan socket according to the provided configuration.

Parameters: - config (SocketcanConfig): Configuration for the CAN connection

Returns: - SocketcanFd: A socket configured for CAN communication

Example: 

from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan

config = SocketcanConfig(channel="can0")
sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)

docs.title: build_send_func(fd: SocketcanFd, *, expects_msg_cls: bool = False) -> SendFn | MessageSendFn
docs.text:
Builds a send function for the given socket.

Parameters: - fd (SocketcanFd): The socket to send messages on - expects_msg_cls (bool): If True, the function accepts CanMessageProtocol objects; otherwise accepts arbitration_id, data, and is_extended separately

Returns: - SendFn: A function accepting (arbitration_id, data, is_extended) if expects_msg_cls=False - MessageSendFn: A function accepting a CanMessageProtocol object if expects_msg_cls=True

Example: 

# Simple send
send_func = build_send_func(sock, expects_msg_cls=False)
send_func(0x123, b'\x01\x02\x03\x04', False)

# Message class send
send_func = build_send_func(sock, expects_msg_cls=True)
message = CanMessage(arbitration_id=0x456, data=b'\x05\x06\x07\x08', is_extended_id=False, timestamp=0.0)
send_func(message)

docs.title: build_recv_func(fd: SocketcanFd, *, use_native_timestamps: bool = True, is_stream: bool = False) -> RecvFn
docs.text:
Builds a receive function for the given socket.

Parameters: - fd (SocketcanFd): The socket to receive messages from - use_native_timestamps (bool): Use hardware timestamps when available (default: True) - is_stream (bool): Use stream mode for daemon-based connections (default: False)

Returns: - RecvFn: A callable that returns a CanMessage when called

Example: 

recv_func = build_recv_func(sock)
message = recv_func()
print(f"Received: ID={message.arbitration_id:#x}, Data={message.data.hex()}")

docs.title: SocketcanConfig
docs.text:
Configuration options for a SocketCAN connection.

Attribute Type Default Description channel str "can0" CAN interface channel name loopback LoopbackMode LoopbackMode.FOR_OTHER_SOCKS Loopback mode for sent messages

docs.title: CanMessage
docs.text:
Container for CAN message data.

Attribute Type Description arbitration_id int CAN message arbitration ID data bytes CAN message payload data is_extended_id bool Whether the arbitration ID is extended (29-bit) timestamp float Message timestamp (seconds since epoch)

docs.title: LoopbackMode
docs.text:
Controls whether transmitted messages are received by the sender or other sockets.

Value Description OFF Sent messages are not received by any socket FOR_OTHER_SOCKS (Default) Sent messages are received by other sockets on the same CAN device, but not the sender ON Sent messages are received by all sockets including the sender

docs.title: SocketcanServer
docs.text:
Listens to a CAN bus and dispatches messages to subscribed consumers.

Constructor: 

SocketcanServer(
    bus: BusABC | None = None,
    *,
    use_native_timestamps: bool = False,
    use_stream: bool = False,
)

Key Methods: - subscribe(filters: set[int] | None = None) -> SocketcanFd: Subscribe to the bus, returns a socketcan-like socket - start(direction: ServerDirection = ServerDirection.BIDIRECTIONAL): Start TX/RX threads - stop(): Stop the server

Example: 

from sockcan.daemon import SocketcanServer, BusParameters

with SocketcanServer.factory(BusParameters(channel="can0", interface="pcan")) as server:
    sock = server.subscribe()
    # Use sock with build_send_func and build_recv_func

docs.title: SocketcanDaemon
docs.text:
HTTP-based daemon that provides SocketCAN service over the network.

Constructor: 

SocketcanDaemon(host: str = "localhost", port: int = 8000)

Key Methods: - register_bus(channel, interface, bitrate=500000, *, use_native_timestamps=False): Register a real CAN bus - register_virtual_bus(channel="vcan0"): Register a virtual CAN bus - start(): Start the daemon - stop(): Stop the daemon

docs.title: BusParameters
docs.text:
Parameters for configuring a CAN bus connection.

Attribute Type Default Description channel str "PCAN_USBBUS1" CAN interface channel interface str "pcan" python-can interface name bitrate int 500000 CAN bus bitrate in bits/second

docs.title: ServerDirection
docs.text:
Controls which directions the socketcan server operates in.

Value Description RX_ONLY Only receive messages from the bus TX_ONLY Only transmit messages to the bus BIDIRECTIONAL (Default) Both receive and transmit

docs.title: hijack_python_can(system: str | None = None) -> None
docs.text:
Permanently overrides python-can's socketcan implementation with Sockcan's faster version.

!!! warning This function modifies global shared state in python-can.

Example: 

import can
from sockcan.interop import hijack_python_can

hijack_python_can()

# Now uses Sockcan's optimized implementation
bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="can0")

docs.title: override_python_can(bus_cls: type[FastSocketcanBus | UserspaceSocketcanBus] = FastSocketcanBus) -> contextmanager
docs.text:
Temporarily overrides python-can's implementation within a context scope.

Example: 

from sockcan.interop import override_python_can

with override_python_can():
    # python-can uses Sockcan here
    bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="can0")

docs.title: activate_userspace_socketcan(parameters, config=None, *, system=None) -> None
docs.text:
Enables userspace SocketCAN on non-Linux platforms (primarily Windows).

!!! warning This function modifies global shared state in python-can.

Example: 

from sockcan.daemon import BusParameters
from sockcan.interop import activate_userspace_socketcan, SocketcanDaemonConfig

config = SocketcanDaemonConfig(
    mode="daemon",
    allow_run_daemon_locally=True,
)
activate_userspace_socketcan(
    BusParameters(channel="PCAN_USBBUS1", interface="pcan"),
    config=config,
)

docs.title: FastSocketcanBus
docs.text:
A high-performance socketcan bus implementation that replaces python-can's default.

Constructor: 

FastSocketcanBus(
    channel: Channel,
    can_filters: CanFilters | None = None,
    **kwargs,
)

docs.title: UserspaceSocketcanBus
docs.text:
A socketcan bus implementation for non-Linux platforms using the userspace daemon.

Constructor: 

UserspaceSocketcanBus(
    channel: Channel,
    can_filters: CanFilters | None = None,
    socket: SocketcanFd | None = None,
    **kwargs,
)

docs.title: SocketcanDaemonConfig
docs.text:
Configuration for the userspace SocketCAN daemon mode.

Attribute Type Default Description mode ServerMode "local" Operating mode: "local" (thread) or "daemon" (HTTP server) allow_run_daemon_locally bool True Allow starting the daemon in the current process host str "localhost" Daemon HTTP server host port int 8000 Daemon HTTP server port linux_too bool False Allow userspace mode on Linux use_native_timestamps bool False Use hardware timestamps

docs.title: Error Codes
docs.text: Sockcan uses standard OS-level error codes and python-can exceptions. This page documents common error scenarios and how to handle them.

docs.title: OS-Level Errors
docs.text: Sockcan is built on top of Python's socket module. Most errors will be standard OS-level errors raised as Python exceptions.

docs.title: OSError
docs.text:
Base class for socket-related errors. Raised when socket operations fail.

Common OSError Subclasses:

Exception errno Description ConnectionRefusedError ECONNREFUSED Connection refused by the daemon ConnectionResetError ECONNRESET Connection was reset by the peer BrokenPipeError EPIPE Write to a closed connection NotConnectedError ENOTCONN Socket is not connected

docs.title: Receiving Errors
docs.text:
When receiving messages, OSError may be raised with the following strerror messages:

Error Message Cause Resolution "No such device" Invalid CAN channel name Verify the channel exists with ip link show "Network is down" CAN interface is not up Bring interface up with ip link set up can0 "Device not configured" Channel doesn't exist Create virtual CAN or check hardware connection

docs.title: Sending Errors
docs.text:
When transmitting messages, errors typically indicate:

Error Cause Resolution OSError on send CAN bus is in error state Check wiring and termination OSError with "Message too long" Data exceeds 8 bytes (Classical CAN) Use CAN-FD or ensure data length ≤ 8

docs.title: python-can Exceptions
docs.text: When using the interoperability layer with python-can, you may encounter these exceptions from the can module:

docs.title: can.CanError
docs.text: Base class for all python-can errors.

docs.title: can.CanOperationError
docs.text:
Raised when a CAN operation fails (transmission error, bus off, etc.).

Common Causes: - Bus is in error state or bus-off - Hardware disconnection - Invalid message parameters

docs.title: can.CanInitializationError
docs.text:
Raised when bus initialization fails.

Common Causes: - Invalid channel name - Interface not available - Insufficient permissions

docs.title: Runtime Errors
docs.text: Error Description RuntimeError: Socketcanserver is not started on channel {channel} No server running for the requested channel in local mode RuntimeError: Daemon mode is used, but no daemon is running Daemon mode selected but daemon is not accessible RuntimeError: Already started Attempting to start an already running server RuntimeError: Tried to enable userspace on Linux Userspace mode requires linux_too=True on Linux

docs.title: Value Errors
docs.text: Error Description ValueError: Native timestamps are not supported on windows use_native_timestamps=True on Windows ValueError: Cannot use socketcan on Windows hijack_python_can() called on Windows

docs.title: Daemon Error Codes
docs.text:
The SocketcanDaemon uses HTTP status codes for communication:

Status Meaning 200 Success 400 Bad request (missing channel parameter, channel not registered) 404 Endpoint not found 101 Switching protocols (WebSocket upgrade for socketcan)

docs.title: Recommended Pattern
docs.text:
import errno
import socket
from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan, build_recv_func

config = SocketcanConfig(channel="can0")
sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)
recv_func = build_recv_func(sock)

try:
    msg = recv_func()
except OSError as e:
    if e.errno == errno.ENODEV:  # No such device
        print("Channel does not exist")
    elif e.errno == errno.ENETDOWN:  # Network is down
        print("CAN interface is not up")
    else:
        raise

docs.title: Graceful Degradation
docs.text:
For production applications, implement reconnection logic:

import time

def connect_with_retry(channel, max_retries=5, delay=1.0):
    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        try:
            config = SocketcanConfig(channel=channel)
            return connect_to_socketcan(config)
        except OSError as e:
            if attempt < max_retries - 1:
                time.sleep(delay)
            else:
                raise RuntimeError(f"Failed to connect after {max_retries} attempts") from e

docs.title: Error Logging
docs.text:
Sockcan uses the logging module with the logger name sockcan. Enable debug logging to troubleshoot issues:

import logging

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)
logging.getLogger("sockcan").setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This tutorial will guide you through setting up and using Sockcan for the first time.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Install Sockcan using pip:

pip install sockcan

For development versions:

pip install git+https://github.com/your-repo/sockcan.git

docs.title: Standalone Usage (Linux with SocketCAN)
docs.text:
The simplest way to use Sockcan on Linux:

from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan, build_send_func, build_recv_func

# Connect to CAN interface
config = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0")
sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)

# Build send and receive functions
send_func = build_send_func(sock, expects_msg_cls=False)
recv_func = build_recv_func(sock)

# Send a message
send_func(0x123, b'\x01\x02\x03\x04', False)

# Receive a message
msg = recv_func()
print(f"ID: {msg.arbitration_id:#x}, Data: {msg.data.hex()}")

docs.title: Linux: Virtual CAN (vcan0)
docs.text:
For testing without hardware:

sudo modprobe vcan
sudo ip link add dev vcan0 type vcan
sudo ip link set up vcan0

docs.title: Linux: Real CAN Hardware
docs.text:
Connect to a physical CAN interface:

# List available interfaces
ip link show

# Bring up your CAN interface
sudo ip link set up can0

docs.title: Windows
docs.text: Windows requires the userspace daemon mode (see Windows Setup).

docs.title: Two Usage Modes
docs.text: Sockcan can be used in two different ways:

docs.title: Mode 1: Standalone
docs.text:
Direct use of Sockcan's API for maximum performance and control.

Best for: - Performance-critical applications - Direct socketcan access on Linux - Applications that don't use python-can

from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan, build_send_func, build_recv_func

config = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0")
sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)
send = build_send_func(sock, expects_msg_cls=False)
recv = build_recv_func(sock)

docs.title: Mode 2: python-can Drop-in Replacement
docs.text:
Use Sockcan as a faster replacement for python-can's socketcan backend.

Best for: - Existing python-can applications - Cross-platform applications - When you want python-can's abstraction layer

import can
from sockcan.interop import hijack_python_can

# Replace python-can's socketcan implementation
hijack_python_can()

# Use python-can as usual
bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="can0")
msg = bus.recv()
bus.send(can.Message(arbitration_id=0x123, data=[1, 2, 3, 4]))

docs.title: Transmitter (sender.py)
docs.text:
from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan, build_send_func
import time

config = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0")
sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)
send_func = build_send_func(sock, expects_msg_cls=False)

try:
    counter = 0
    while True:
        data = bytes([counter % 256])
        send_func(0x100, data, False)
        print(f"Sent: ID=0x100, Data={data.hex()}")
        counter += 1
        time.sleep(1)
finally:
    sock.close()

docs.title: Receiver (receiver.py)
docs.text:
from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan, build_recv_func

config = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0")
sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)
recv_func = build_recv_func(sock)

try:
    print("Waiting for messages...")
    while True:
        msg = recv_func()
        print(f"Received: ID={msg.arbitration_id:#x}, Data={msg.data.hex()}")
finally:
    sock.close()

docs.title: Running the Example
docs.text:
Open two terminals:

# Terminal 1: Start receiver
python receiver.py

# Terminal 2: Start transmitter
python sender.py

You should see messages being transmitted and received in real-time.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Learn about core concepts to understand how Sockcan works
  • Explore basic usage for more examples
  • Set up virtual CAN for testing
  • Configure Windows support for cross-platform development

docs.title: Virtual CAN on Linux
docs.text: Virtual CAN (vcan) allows you to create software-only CAN buses for testing and development without physical hardware.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Ensure the vcan kernel module is loaded:

sudo modprobe vcan

docs.title: Creating a Virtual Interface
docs.text:
Create a virtual CAN interface named vcan0:

sudo ip link add dev vcan0 type vcan
sudo ip link set up vcan0

docs.title: Verifying the Setup
docs.text:
Check that the interface was created:

ip link show vcan0

You should see output similar to:

5: vcan0: <NOARP,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 16 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN mode DEFAULT
    link/can

docs.title: Standalone Mode
docs.text:
Create a virtual CAN network with multiple applications:

# app1.py - Receiver
from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan, build_recv_func

config = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0")
sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)
recv = build_recv_func(sock)

print("Waiting for messages on vcan0...")
msg = recv()
print(f"Received: ID={msg.arbitration_id:#x}, Data={msg.data.hex()}")

# app2.py - Sender
from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan, build_send_func

config = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0")
sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)
send = build_send_func(sock, expects_msg_cls=False)

send(0x123, b'\xde\xad\xbe\xef', False)
print("Message sent!")

Run both applications in separate terminals - messages will be transmitted between them.

docs.title: python-can Interoperability Layer
docs.text:
Use virtual CAN with python-can's interface:

import can
from sockcan.interop import hijack_python_can

hijack_python_can()

bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="vcan0")

# Send a message
msg = can.Message(
    arbitration_id=0x456,
    data=[1, 2, 3, 4],
    is_extended_id=False,
)
bus.send(msg)

# Receive a message
received = bus.recv(timeout=1.0)
print(f"Received: {received}")

docs.title: Using Real CAN Hardware
docs.text: When you have real CAN hardware connected (e.g., on interface can0), simply change the channel name:

docs.title: Standalone Mode
docs.text:
from sockcan import SocketcanConfig, connect_to_socketcan, build_send_func

config = SocketcanConfig(channel="can0")  # Use real interface
sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)
send = build_send_func(sock, expects_msg_cls=False)

send(0x100, b'\x01\x02\x03\x04', False)
print("Message sent to real CAN bus!")

docs.title: python-can Interoperability Layer
docs.text:
import can
from sockcan.interop import hijack_python_can

hijack_python_can()

bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="can0")
msg = can.Message(arbitration_id=0x789, data=[0x11, 0x22, 0x33])
bus.send(msg)

docs.title: Multiple Virtual CAN Interfaces
docs.text:
Create multiple virtual interfaces for complex testing scenarios:

sudo ip link add dev vcan0 type vcan
sudo ip link set up vcan0
sudo ip link add dev vcan1 type vcan
sudo ip link set up vcan1

docs.title: Isolated Testing
docs.text:
Applications on different vcan interfaces are isolated from each other:

# app_on_vcan0.py
config = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan0")
sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)

# app_on_vcan1.py
config = SocketcanConfig(channel="vcan1")
sock = connect_to_socketcan(config)

docs.title: Bridging Virtual and Physical CAN
docs.text:
Use can-utils cangw or the SocketcanServer to bridge virtual and physical CAN:

from sockcan.daemon import SocketcanServer, BusParameters
from sockcan import build_send_func, build_recv_func

# Bridge vcan0 to real CAN hardware on can0
with SocketcanServer.factory(
    BusParameters(channel="can0", interface="socketcan")
) as server:
    vcan_sock = server.subscribe()
    recv = build_recv_func(vcan_sock)
    send = build_send_func(vcan_sock, expects_msg_cls=False)

    # Messages from can0 appear on vcan_sock
    msg = recv()

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
Remove a virtual CAN interface when no longer needed:

sudo ip link delete vcan0

Or remove all vcan interfaces:

sudo ip link show | grep vcan | cut -d: -f2 | xargs -I{} sudo ip link delete {}

docs.title: Interface Not Found
docs.text:
OSError: [Errno 19] No such device

Solution: Create the virtual interface first: 

sudo modprobe vcan
sudo ip link add dev vcan0 type vcan
sudo ip link set up vcan0

docs.title: Permission Denied
docs.text:
PermissionError: [Errno 1] Operation not permitted

Solution: Run with sudo or add your user to the can group: 

sudo usermod -aG can $USER

docs.title: Messages Not Received
docs.text:
Possible causes: 1. Interface not in UP state 2. Loopback mode is OFF 3. Different channel names

Solution: Verify interface state: 

ip link show vcan0
# Should show: <NOARP,UP,LOWER_UP>

docs.title: Windows Setup
docs.text: Sockcan provides userspace SocketCAN support on Windows through a daemon-based architecture. This guide covers setting up Sockcan on Windows systems.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
On Windows, Sockcan uses a daemon-based architecture:

  1. A daemon runs locally, connecting to your physical CAN hardware (e.g., PCAN)
  2. Sockcan's userspace implementation provides SocketCAN-compatible sockets
  3. Your application connects to the daemon via the interoperability layer

This allows existing SocketCAN code to work on Windows with minimal changes.

docs.title: 1. Install a CAN Hardware Driver
docs.text:
Windows doesn't have native SocketCAN support. You'll need:

  • PCAN-USB: Install PEAK-System drivers
  • Other interfaces: Ensure python-can supports your hardware

docs.title: 2. Install python-can
docs.text: pip install python-can

docs.title: 3. Install Sockcan
docs.text: pip install sockcan

docs.title: Standalone Mode (with Daemon)
docs.text:
Create a local server that bridges your physical CAN hardware:

from sockcan.daemon import SocketcanServer, BusParameters, SocketcanDaemon
from sockcan import build_send_func, build_recv_func

# Create daemon with your PCAN hardware
with SocketcanDaemon() as daemon:
    daemon.register_bus(
        channel="PCAN_USBBUS1",
        interface="pcan",
        bitrate=500000,
    )
    daemon.start()

    # Get a socketcan-like socket from the server
    sock = daemon._servers["PCAN_USBBUS1"].subscribe()

    # Use standard SocketCAN API
    send = build_send_func(sock, expects_msg_cls=False)
    recv = build_recv_func(sock)

    send(0x100, b'\x01\x02\x03\x04', False)
    msg = recv()

docs.title: python-can Drop-in Replacement (Recommended)
docs.text:
The recommended approach for Windows is using the interoperability layer:

from sockcan.daemon import BusParameters
from sockcan.interop import activate_userspace_socketcan, SocketcanDaemonConfig
import can

# Configure for local daemon mode
config = SocketcanDaemonConfig(
    mode="local",
    allow_run_daemon_locally=True,
)

# Activate userspace socketcan with your hardware
activate_userspace_socketcan(
    BusParameters(
        channel="PCAN_USBBUS1",
        interface="pcan",
        bitrate=500000,
    ),
    config=config,
)

# Now use python-can as usual - it automatically uses Sockcan's implementation
bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="PCAN_USBBUS1")
msg = bus.recv()
bus.send(can.Message(arbitration_id=0x123, data=[1, 2, 3, 4]))

docs.title: Local Mode (Default)
docs.text:
The daemon runs in a local thread within your application:

from sockcan.interop import activate_userspace_socketcan, SocketcanDaemonConfig
from sockcan.daemon import BusParameters

config = SocketcanDaemonConfig(
    mode="local",
    allow_run_daemon_locally=True,
)

activate_userspace_socketcan(
    BusParameters(channel="PCAN_USBBUS1", interface="pcan"),
    config=config,
)

Pros: - Simple setup - Automatic cleanup on exit - No separate process

Cons: - Daemon runs in your application process - Less suitable for multi-application scenarios

docs.title: Daemon Mode
docs.text:
Run a separate daemon process accessible via HTTP:

from sockcan.interop import activate_userspace_socketcan, SocketcanDaemonConfig
from sockcan.daemon import BusParameters, ensure_socketcan_daemon_running

config = SocketcanDaemonConfig(
    mode="daemon",
    host="localhost",
    port=8000,
    allow_run_daemon_locally=True,
)

daemon = ensure_socketcan_daemon_running(host="localhost", port=8000)

activate_userspace_socketcan(
    BusParameters(channel="PCAN_USBBUS1", interface="pcan"),
    config=config,
)

Pros: - Separate process (doesn't block application) - Multiple applications can share the same daemon - Remote access possible

Cons: - More complex setup - Requires daemon process management

docs.title: Using Real CAN Hardware
docs.text: On Windows, always use the interoperability layer with your hardware:

docs.title: Example: PCAN-USB
docs.text:
from sockcan.interop import activate_userspace_socketcan, SocketcanDaemonConfig
from sockcan.daemon import BusParameters
import can

activate_userspace_socketcan(
    BusParameters(
        channel="PCAN_USBBUS1",  # Common PCAN channel name
        interface="pcan",
        bitrate=500000,
    ),
)

bus = can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel="PCAN_USBBUS1")

# Your existing SocketCAN code works unchanged
msg = can.Message(arbitration_id=0x7E8, data=[0x02, 0x01, 0x0D, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00])
bus.send(msg)

response = bus.recv(timeout=1.0)
print(f"Response: {response}")

docs.title: Example: Other Interfaces
docs.text:
# Vector CANalyzer
activate_userspace_socketcan(
    BusParameters(channel="CAN1", interface="vector", bitrate=500000),
)

# Kvaser
activate_userspace_socketcan(
    BusParameters(channel="0", interface="kvaser", bitrate=500000),
)

docs.title: Cross-Platform Development
docs.text:
Sockcan enables writing platform-agnostic CAN code:

import platform
from sockcan.interop import activate_userspace_socketcan, SocketcanDaemonConfig
from sockcan.daemon import BusParameters
import can

def setup_can(channel, interface, bitrate=500000):
    """Platform-aware CAN setup."""
    system = platform.system()

    if system == "Windows":
        # Windows: Use userspace daemon
        config = SocketcanDaemonConfig(mode="local")
        activate_userspace_socketcan(
            BusParameters(channel=channel, interface=interface, bitrate=bitrate),
            config=config,
        )
    else:
        # Linux/macOS: Hijack python-can's socketcan
        from sockcan.interop import hijack_python_can
        hijack_python_can()

    return can.Bus(interface="socketcan", channel=channel)

# Works on both Windows and Linux!
bus = setup_can("PCAN_USBBUS1", "pcan")
bus.send(can.Message(arbitration_id=0x100, data=[1, 2, 3, 4]))

docs.title: Virtual CAN on Windows
docs.text:
Windows doesn't support virtual CAN interfaces natively. However, you can simulate virtual CAN using the daemon:

from sockcan.interop import activate_userspace_socketcan, SocketcanDaemonConfig
from sockcan.daemon import BusParameters
from sockcan import build_send_func, build_recv_func

# Register a virtual bus (no real hardware)
config = SocketcanDaemonConfig(mode="local")

# First, register with a dummy interface to enable the virtual bus
activate_userspace_socketcan(
    parameters=None,  # No real bus parameters
    config=config,
)

# Create virtual server manually
from sockcan.daemon import SocketcanServer

server = SocketcanServer(use_stream=True)  # Virtual mode
server.start()

sock = server.subscribe()
send = build_send_func(sock, expects_msg_cls=False)
recv = build_recv_func(sock)

# Simulate CAN communication
send(0x123, b'\xaa\xbb\xcc\xdd', False)
msg = recv()

docs.title: No Hardware Detected
docs.text:
RuntimeError: No PCAN devices found

Solution: 1. Verify hardware is connected and powered 2. Install correct drivers 3. Check device appears in Device Manager

docs.title: Daemon Connection Failed
docs.text:
RuntimeError: Daemon mode is used, but no daemon is running

Solution: 

from sockcan.daemon import ensure_socketcan_daemon_running
ensure_socketcan_daemon_running()

docs.title: Channel Not Found
docs.text:
ValueError: Channel PCAN_USBBUS1 not found

Solution: 1. Verify the channel name matches your hardware 2. List available channels with your CAN driver's tools

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
On Windows, the daemon-based approach may introduce slight latency compared to native Linux SocketCAN. For minimum latency:

  1. Use local mode instead of daemon mode
  2. Reduce network hops (use localhost for daemon mode)
  3. Use SOCK_STREAM mode for streaming applications:
from sockcan.daemon import SocketcanServer

server = SocketcanServer(use_stream=True)  # Lower latency
